1.synthesis: The hand of the LORD brings Ezekiel out in the Spirit and sets him down in the middle of a valley full of bones (v.1). He is led all around them, and behold, “there were very many… and behold, they were very dry” (v.2). The integrative thread is the staggering image of total death that opens the most famous vision of resurrection hope: before any word of life is spoken, the prophet is made to survey a valley of scattered, sun-bleached bones — “very many” and “very dry” — confronting him with the utter hopelessness of Israel’s condition in exile, so that the life God will speak comes manifestly from God alone, raising what is beyond all human remedy.
1.historical_context: The valley of dry bones (vv.1–2) likely evokes an ancient battlefield where the slain lay unburied, their bones picked clean and bleached — an image of catastrophic defeat. “In the Spirit” (v.1) marks this as a visionary experience like the temple visions (Ezekiel 8:3). The emphasis that the bones were “very many” and “very dry” (v.2) underscores the totality and finality of death, setting the stage for a restoration that can only be a divine act of new creation.
1.literary_structure: These opening verses (vv.1–2) set the scene for the dry-bones vision, the prophet’s guided tour around the bones (v.2) heightening the sense of pervasive, hopeless death before the command to prophesy. The doubled “very” — very many, very dry — is deliberate rhetorical emphasis, framing the miracle of life that follows as wholly the work of God.
1.theological_application: God often begins his greatest works by confronting us with the full extent of our deadness (v.2; Ephesians 2:1). The valley of very dry bones pictures every situation that is humanly beyond hope — dead faith, a dead church, a dead soul. The believer learns not to measure God’s power by the depth of the hopelessness before him, for the One who surveys the dry bones is about to speak them to life. Honest reckoning with death is the threshold of resurrection hope, driving us to look to God alone for life.
1.christology: The valley of death surveyed before life is spoken (v.2) anticipates the gospel pattern in which Christ confronts the tomb — “Lazarus had already been in the tomb four days” (John 11:17) — before calling forth life. He is the resurrection and the life (John 11:25) who alone can speak to the very dry bones of a dead humanity. The hopeless valley magnifies the One who “gives life to the dead and calls into existence the things that do not exist” (Romans 4:17).

3.synthesis: God asks, “Son of man, can these bones live?” and Ezekiel answers, “O Lord GOD, you know” (v.3). Commanded to prophesy, he speaks God’s word over the bones: “Behold, I will cause breath to enter you, and you shall live” (vv.4–6). As he prophesies, there is a rattling, and the bones come together, sinews and flesh and skin cover them — but there is no breath in them (vv.7–8). Then God commands him to prophesy to the breath, and as he does, “the breath came into them, and they lived and stood on their feet, an exceedingly great army” (vv.9–10). The integrative thread is resurrection accomplished through the prophetic word and the breath of God: life comes not by human power but as Ezekiel proclaims God’s word and the divine breath (ruach/Spirit) enters — a two-stage re-creation echoing Eden, where formed bodies become living beings only when God breathes life, declaring that God raises the dead by his word and Spirit.
3.historical_context: The question “can these bones live?” (v.3) and the prophet’s reverent “you know” acknowledge that only God can answer. The two-stage process — assembly of bodies, then the entrance of breath (vv.7–10) — echoes the creation of Adam, formed from dust then animated by God’s breath (Genesis 2:7). The Hebrew ruach means breath, wind, and Spirit, deliberately exploited here (v.9), and the “exceedingly great army” (v.10) pictures the once-dead nation raised and mustered.
3.literary_structure: This central section (vv.3–10) narrates the miracle in two prophetic commands — first to the bones (v.4), then to the breath (v.9) — building dramatic tension between the reassembled-but-lifeless bodies (v.8) and the inrushing breath that brings them to life. The play on ruach (breath/wind/Spirit) is the structural and theological key, and the standing army (v.10) is the climactic result.
3.theological_application: God raises the dead through the proclamation of his word empowered by his Spirit (v.4, v.9; Romans 10:17). The pattern teaches that the preached word, however unlikely its hearers, is the instrument by which God gives life, and that outward reformation without the breath of the Spirit is still lifeless (v.8; 2 Timothy 3:5). The believer is encouraged to keep prophesying — speaking God’s word — over situations that seem dead, trusting the Spirit to do what no human strength can, and to seek the animating breath of God, not mere external religion.
3.christology: The life-giving word and breath (v.5) are fulfilled in Christ, whose words “are spirit and life” (John 6:63) and who breathed on his disciples saying “receive the Holy Spirit” (John 20:22), echoing the breath that raised the bones. The risen Christ is the firstfruits of the resurrection this vision foreshadows (1 Corinthians 15:20), and at his word the dead will hear and live (John 5:25). The two-stage animation points to him as both the second Adam and the life-giving Spirit (1 Corinthians 15:45).

11.pericope_label: These Bones Are the House of Israel
11.synthesis: God interprets the vision: “these bones are the whole house of Israel,” who say, “Our bones are dried up, and our hope is lost; we are indeed cut off” (v.11). Therefore God promises, “I will open your graves and raise you from your graves, O my people. And I will bring you into the land of Israel” (v.12); “you shall know that I am the LORD” when he opens the graves (v.13), and he will put his Spirit within them so they live and dwell in their own land (v.14). The integrative thread is the application of the resurrection vision to a despairing nation: the dry bones are Israel’s lost hope in exile, and God answers their despair — “our hope is lost; we are cut off” — with the promise to open graves and give his Spirit, transforming the vision into a pledge of national restoration that simultaneously plants the seed of resurrection hope for the individual.
11.historical_context: The interpretation (v.11) identifies the bones as the exiled house of Israel, whose lament “our hope is lost; we are cut off” captures the despair of the captivity. The grave-opening language (vv.12–13) is metaphorical for return from exile, yet its imagery feeds the developing biblical hope of bodily resurrection (Daniel 12:2; Isaiah 26:19). The promise to put God’s Spirit within them (v.14) links the vision to the new-covenant gift of chapter 36.
11.literary_structure: This interpretive section (vv.11–14) decodes the vision, the people’s despairing lament (v.11) answered by God’s grave-opening promise (v.12). The repeated recognition formula and the gift of the Spirit (v.14) bind the vision to the surrounding restoration oracles, turning a picture into a covenant pledge.
11.theological_application: God meets the despair of those who say “our hope is lost” (v.11) with resurrection power, for no situation is too far gone for the One who opens graves (v.12). The promise teaches that hope for God’s people rests not in their own vitality but in his Spirit-given life. The believer facing spiritual deadness, or the grave itself, is assured that God raises the hopeless and the dead, and that the same Spirit who will raise our bodies already dwells within as the guarantee (Romans 8:11).
11.christology: God’s promise “I will open your graves” (v.12) is fulfilled in Christ, at whose death the tombs were opened and many bodies of the saints were raised (Matthew 27:52–53), and who declares the hour when “all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out” (John 5:28–29). The despairing cry “we are cut off” is answered by the One who was himself “cut off out of the land of the living” (Isaiah 53:8) and raised, so that in him the hopeless have a living hope through his resurrection (1 Peter 1:3).

15.synthesis: God commands Ezekiel a sign-act: take one stick and write on it “For Judah,” and another and write “For Joseph (Ephraim),” representing the divided kingdoms (vv.15–16). Then “join them one to another into one stick, that they may become one in your hand” (v.17). The integrative thread is the enacted promise of reunion through a tangible sign: the long-divided people of God — the northern and southern kingdoms severed since the days after Solomon — are dramatized as two sticks that God joins into one in the prophet’s hand, a vivid sign-act declaring that the restoration God intends is not only resurrection from death but the healing of division, gathering the scattered tribes into a single, undivided people under one King.
15.historical_context: The two sticks (vv.15–16) represent the kingdoms split since the division after Solomon (1 Kings 12): Judah (the southern kingdom) and Joseph/Ephraim (the northern kingdom, long since exiled by Assyria). Sign-acts were a regular feature of Ezekiel’s ministry (Ezekiel 4–5; Ezekiel 12). Joining them “into one stick” (v.17) enacts the reunification of all twelve tribes, a hope long held among the prophets (Jeremiah 3:18; Hosea 1:11). The act of writing the names on the sticks (v.16) makes the sign legible to onlookers, much as a deed or staff might be inscribed to mark ownership; the joined stick held aloft “in your hand” (v.17) becomes a public, visible pledge that what God declares he will surely perform.
15.literary_structure: This sign-act (vv.15–17) introduces the theme of reunion, the two inscribed sticks joined in the prophet’s hand (v.17) forming the visible sign that the explanatory oracle will unfold. Placed after the dry-bones vision, it extends the restoration from resurrection of the dead to the reunification of the divided, preparing for the promise of one king over one nation.
15.theological_application: God’s restoration heals division as well as death: the two sticks made one (v.17) declare his purpose to gather his scattered people into one (John 11:52). The sign warns against the scandal of a fractured people of God and points to the unity he himself creates. The believer is reminded that true unity is God’s work, joining what sin has divided, and is called to pursue “the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace” (Ephesians 4:3) as a reflection of the one people God is forming under one King.
15.christology: The joining of the two sticks into one (v.17) is fulfilled in Christ, who gathers into one the children of God who were scattered (John 11:52) and makes Jew and Gentile “one new man” by abolishing the dividing wall (Ephesians 2:14–16). He is the one Shepherd of the one flock (John 10:16) in whom the long-divided people of God are united, the true King under whom the reunited tribes are gathered into a single, everlasting kingdom. The single stick held in the prophet’s hand foreshadows the cross, the wood on which the dividing enmity was slain, by which the scattered are drawn together into one body (Colossians 2:14; John 12:32).

16.pericope_label: I Will Unite Them into One Nation
16.synthesis: God explains the sign: he will take the stick of Joseph and the stick of Judah and make them one in his hand (vv.18–19). He will gather the people of Israel from among the nations, bring them to their own land, and make them “one nation… and one king shall be king over them all,” no longer two kingdoms nor defiled by idols (vv.20–23). “My servant David shall be king over them, and they shall all have one shepherd” (v.24); they will obey his rules and dwell in the land forever, and God will make “a covenant of peace… an everlasting covenant,” set his sanctuary in their midst forever, and be their God (vv.25–28). The integrative thread is the consummate restoration: one people, one Davidic King, an everlasting covenant of peace, and God dwelling among them forever — the reunion sign unfolds into the fullest promise of the book, in which cleansing, the messianic shepherd-king, perpetual obedience, and God’s abiding sanctuary converge in a kingdom that will never end.
16.historical_context: The explanation (v.19) interprets the two sticks as the reunited kingdoms gathered from exile into one nation under one king (v.22). “My servant David” as everlasting king and one shepherd (vv.24–25) is the messianic Davidic hope of Ezekiel 34:23 and 2 Samuel 7:16. The “everlasting covenant” and God’s sanctuary set among them forever (vv.26–28) anticipate both the new covenant and the visionary temple of chapters 40–48, with the covenant formula sealing the relationship.
16.literary_structure: This concluding section (vv.18–28) unfolds the sign-act into the climactic restoration promise, moving from reunion (v.19) to one king (v.24) to everlasting covenant and indwelling sanctuary (vv.26–28). It gathers the book’s major hopes — regathering, cleansing, the Davidic shepherd, the covenant of peace, and God’s dwelling presence — into a single crescendo that prepares for the temple vision to come.
16.theological_application: God’s saving purpose culminates in one redeemed people, ruled by the messianic King, kept in obedience, and indwelt by God’s own presence forever (vv.24–28). The “everlasting covenant of peace” assures the believer that this restoration is permanent and unbreakable, grounded in God’s commitment, not human faithfulness (v.26; Hebrews 13:20). The promise that God will set his sanctuary among them and be their God (v.27) is the goal of all redemption — God dwelling with his people — toward which the believer’s hope is directed.
16.christology: “My servant David… one shepherd” reigning forever (vv.24–25) is Jesus, the Son of David and one Shepherd (John 10:16) whose kingdom has no end (Luke 1:32–33). The everlasting covenant of peace is ratified in his blood (Hebrews 13:20), and God’s promise “my dwelling place shall be with them” (v.27) is fulfilled first in the incarnate Word who tabernacled among us (John 1:14) and finally in the new Jerusalem, where God dwells with his people and is their God (Revelation 21:3; 2 Corinthians 6:16).
